channel.title: torontotrees
channel.description: Essays and visualizations built on Toronto's open street-tree data.

item.title: A tale of two Victorians — Cabbagetown vs. Trinity-Bellwoods
item.description: Two old downtown Toronto neighbourhoods, same era, same rowhouse DNA, same gentrification arc. One has 45% canopy. The other has 20%. Here

item.title: The west-end cherry belt — where to see cherry blossoms in Toronto
item.description: High Park is famous for cherry blossoms. But the data says Oakwood Village has more — and nine times as many cherries line the west-end blocks around St Clair.

item.title: The honey locust — Toronto's civic workhorse
Beyond the curb — Toronto's actual canopy
Every street tree in Toronto, as a dot
item.description: A pointillist portrait of Toronto: 689,013 city-owned street trees, coloured by genus. Plus a map showing just the 69,563 Norway maples — and why that matters.

item.title: Toronto's rarest street trees — a pilgrimage guide
item.description: Seven tree species have exactly one specimen in all of Toronto

item.title: The black locust — Toronto's 2,943 Robinia pseudoacacia
item.description: Black locust is one of Toronto

item.title: A decade of standing still — how Toronto's canopy survived the ash catastrophe
item.description: Between 2008 and 2018, Toronto lost an estimated 80,000 mature ash trees to emerald ash borer. Over the same decade, citywide canopy cover went from 25.84% to 25.89%. Here

item.title: Toronto's canopy is unequally shared — torontotrees
item.description: Four findings from an afternoon with Toronto
